title: Subsets II
code: for (int i = start; i < nums.Length; i++)

frames.caption: Sort nums so equal values are adjacent, then DFS the decision tree. We record at EVERY node, and skip a duplicate value when a sibling already branched on it at this level.
frames.note: Subsets II is plain subsets plus one guard. Sorting is what makes the de-dup cheap: equal duplicates sit next to each other, so a single `nums[i] == nums[i-1]` comparison spots a repeated sibling. Without sorting you would need a hash set per level. The output is at most 2^n subsets, so the run is O(n * 2^n).

frames.caption: Enter Backtrack(start=0). Record subset [] (subset #1).
frames.note: Every node of the decision tree is a valid subset, so we record a COPY of the path the moment we enter a call — the empty set, singletons, and full set all qualify. Copying matters: the path buffer keeps mutating as recursion continues, so storing the reference would leave every result aliasing the same eventually-empty list (the classic backtracking bug).

frames.caption: i=0: CHOOSE nums[0]=1 -> path becomes [1]. Explore Backtrack(1).
frames.note: CHOOSE: append nums[i] to the path, then EXPLORE by recursing with start = i+1. Passing i+1 (never i) means we only look forward, so each element is decided once. Going DEEPER with the next equal element is fine (there i == start) — what we forbid is two SIBLINGS at the same level picking the same value.

frames.caption: Enter Backtrack(start=1). Record subset [1] (subset #2).
frames.note: Every node of the decision tree is a valid subset, so we record a COPY of the path the moment we enter a call — the empty set, singletons, and full set all qualify. Copying matters: the path buffer keeps mutating as recursion continues, so storing the reference would leave every result aliasing the same eventually-empty list (the classic backtracking bug).

frames.caption: i=1: CHOOSE nums[1]=2 -> path becomes [1,2]. Explore Backtrack(2).
frames.note: CHOOSE: append nums[i] to the path, then EXPLORE by recursing with start = i+1. Passing i+1 (never i) means we only look forward, so each element is decided once. Going DEEPER with the next equal element is fine (there i == start) — what we forbid is two SIBLINGS at the same level picking the same value.

frames.caption: Enter Backtrack(start=2). Record subset [1,2] (subset #3).
frames.note: Every node of the decision tree is a valid subset, so we record a COPY of the path the moment we enter a call — the empty set, singletons, and full set all qualify. Copying matters: the path buffer keeps mutating as recursion continues, so storing the reference would leave every result aliasing the same eventually-empty list (the classic backtracking bug).

frames.caption: i=2: CHOOSE nums[2]=2 -> path becomes [1,2,2]. Explore Backtrack(3).
frames.note: CHOOSE: append nums[i] to the path, then EXPLORE by recursing with start = i+1. Passing i+1 (never i) means we only look forward, so each element is decided once. Going DEEPER with the next equal element is fine (there i == start) — what we forbid is two SIBLINGS at the same level picking the same value.

frames.caption: Enter Backtrack(start=3). Record subset [1,2,2] (subset #4).
frames.note: Every node of the decision tree is a valid subset, so we record a COPY of the path the moment we enter a call — the empty set, singletons, and full set all qualify. Copying matters: the path buffer keeps mutating as recursion continues, so storing the reference would leave every result aliasing the same eventually-empty list (the classic backtracking bug).

frames.caption: Back at start=2, i=2: UN-CHOOSE 2 -> path is [1,2] again. Continue to the next sibling.
frames.note: UN-CHOOSE: pop the element back off the path so the next sibling branch starts from a clean slate. Choose -> explore -> un-choose is the universal three-line skeleton; the rewind is what makes it "backtracking".

frames.caption: Back at start=1, i=1: UN-CHOOSE 2 -> path is [1] again. Continue to the next sibling.
frames.note: UN-CHOOSE: pop the element back off the path so the next sibling branch starts from a clean slate. Choose -> explore -> un-choose is the universal three-line skeleton; the rewind is what makes it "backtracking".

frames.caption: i=2: nums[2]=2 == nums[1]=2 and i>start(1) -> SKIP this duplicate sibling.
frames.note: SKIP the duplicate sibling. Because nums is sorted, equal values are adjacent, so `i > start && nums[i] == nums[i-1]` detects "a sibling already branched on this exact value at this level." The first occurrence (i == start) is allowed to branch; the second-and-later equal siblings are pruned — that is the single rule that turns ambiguous duplicates into exactly one subset each.

frames.caption: Back at start=0, i=0: UN-CHOOSE 1 -> path is [] again. Continue to the next sibling.
frames.note: UN-CHOOSE: pop the element back off the path so the next sibling branch starts from a clean slate. Choose -> explore -> un-choose is the universal three-line skeleton; the rewind is what makes it "backtracking".

frames.caption: i=1: CHOOSE nums[1]=2 -> path becomes [2]. Explore Backtrack(2).
frames.note: CHOOSE: append nums[i] to the path, then EXPLORE by recursing with start = i+1. Passing i+1 (never i) means we only look forward, so each element is decided once. Going DEEPER with the next equal element is fine (there i == start) — what we forbid is two SIBLINGS at the same level picking the same value.

frames.caption: Enter Backtrack(start=2). Record subset [2] (subset #5).
frames.note: Every node of the decision tree is a valid subset, so we record a COPY of the path the moment we enter a call — the empty set, singletons, and full set all qualify. Copying matters: the path buffer keeps mutating as recursion continues, so storing the reference would leave every result aliasing the same eventually-empty list (the classic backtracking bug).

frames.caption: i=2: CHOOSE nums[2]=2 -> path becomes [2,2]. Explore Backtrack(3).
frames.note: CHOOSE: append nums[i] to the path, then EXPLORE by recursing with start = i+1. Passing i+1 (never i) means we only look forward, so each element is decided once. Going DEEPER with the next equal element is fine (there i == start) — what we forbid is two SIBLINGS at the same level picking the same value.

frames.caption: Enter Backtrack(start=3). Record subset [2,2] (subset #6).
frames.note: Every node of the decision tree is a valid subset, so we record a COPY of the path the moment we enter a call — the empty set, singletons, and full set all qualify. Copying matters: the path buffer keeps mutating as recursion continues, so storing the reference would leave every result aliasing the same eventually-empty list (the classic backtracking bug).

frames.caption: Back at start=2, i=2: UN-CHOOSE 2 -> path is [2] again. Continue to the next sibling.
frames.note: UN-CHOOSE: pop the element back off the path so the next sibling branch starts from a clean slate. Choose -> explore -> un-choose is the universal three-line skeleton; the rewind is what makes it "backtracking".

frames.caption: Back at start=0, i=1: UN-CHOOSE 2 -> path is [] again. Continue to the next sibling.
frames.note: UN-CHOOSE: pop the element back off the path so the next sibling branch starts from a clean slate. Choose -> explore -> un-choose is the universal three-line skeleton; the rewind is what makes it "backtracking".

frames.caption: i=2: nums[2]=2 == nums[1]=2 and i>start(0) -> SKIP this duplicate sibling.
frames.note: SKIP the duplicate sibling. Because nums is sorted, equal values are adjacent, so `i > start && nums[i] == nums[i-1]` detects "a sibling already branched on this exact value at this level." The first occurrence (i == start) is allowed to branch; the second-and-later equal siblings are pruned — that is the single rule that turns ambiguous duplicates into exactly one subset each.

frames.caption: Done: 6 distinct subsets (< 2^3=8). The skip rule pruned the two branches that would have re-generated [2] and [2] inside [1].
frames.note: With duplicates the answer count drops below 2^n because some include/exclude combinations collapse to the same set. The skip guard removes exactly the redundant sibling branches, so every distinct subset is emitted once and none twice. Space (excluding output) stays O(n) — only one root-to-node path is held at a time.
